introduction: Profit & Loss deals with Cost Price (CP) and Selling Price (SP) relationships. It is a core SSC CGL topic, heavily tested with dishonest shopkeeper (faulty weight), mixture-based, and multi-article combined transactions. Mastering CP:SP ratio conversion is the key speed technique.

concepts.explanation: Profit = SP-CP (when SP>CP); Loss = CP-SP (when CP>SP). Profit%/Loss% always calculated on CP unless stated otherwise (e.g. 'on SP').
Convert profit/loss % into ratio: profit x% => CP:SP=100:(100+x); loss x% => CP:SP=100:(100-x). Solve using ratios instead of decimals for speed.
For combined profit/loss across multiple items, always find Total CP and Total SP separately, then compute overall %.
If profit is stated as % of SP (not CP, e.g. 'gains 1/5 of SP'), convert using SP:Profit:CP ratio and re-derive profit% on CP basis.
When shopkeeper uses less weight while claiming to sell at cost/specific price, effective CP:SP ratio = (true weight):(claimed/faulty weight), e.g. using 950g for 1kg means CP:SP=950:1000.

concepts.concept: Cheating both while buying and selling
concepts.explanation: Combine both cheating ratios by multiplying: overall CP:SP = (buy cheat ratio) x (sell cheat ratio).

concepts.concept: Marked-up then discount (link with Discount topic)
concepts.explanation: SP after markup and discount = CP x (100+markup)/100 x (100-discount)/100; compare with CP for final profit/loss%.

formulas: If SP of two items is EQUAL, one sold at x% profit, other at x% loss => overall LOSS% = x²/100
If CP of N articles = SP of M articles at x% profit => N:M = (100+x):100
Faulty weight profit: CP:SP = (weight used while buying):(weight claimed while selling); profit% derived from this ratio
Successive selling (A to B to C): Final Price = CP x (100+p1%)/100 x (100-l2%)/100 (chain multiplication)
Mixture average CP = (q1xr1 + q2xr2)/(q1+q2); Sale rate for target profit% = Average CP x (100+profit%)/100

question_types.identification_method: Any two of CP, SP, profit%/loss% given, third asked directly.
question_types.when_to_use: Use CP:SP ratio method — convert given % into ratio (100:100+x or 100:100-x) and cross-multiply.
step_by_step: Convert profit%/loss% into CP:SP ratio
Match given value (CP or SP) to the correct ratio term
Cross multiply to find missing value
question_types.shortcut: CP=100, SP=100+profit or 100-loss — use as ratio, avoid decimal multiplication.
common_mistakes: Calculating profit% on SP when it should be on CP (default is always CP unless stated)
Sign confusion between profit and loss ratio direction

practice_questions.q: SP=₹8540, profit=22%. Find CP.
SP=₹8540, loss=22%. Find CP.
CP=₹400, SP=₹420. Find profit%.
CP=₹400, SP=₹390. Find loss%.
SP of 100 pens = CP of 140 pens. Find profit%.
CP of 36 articles = SP of N articles at 20% profit. Find N.

question_types.type_name: Chain Selling (A to B to C)
question_types.identification_method: Article sold from person A to B, then B to C, with different profit/loss% at each stage.
question_types.when_to_use: Chain multiply CP with successive (100+p)/100 or (100-l)/100 for each stage to reach final price.
step_by_step: Write CP (unknown, let it be x) for the first seller
Apply first %change (profit/loss) to get intermediate price
Apply second %change on intermediate price to get final price
Equate final price to given value and solve for x
question_types.shortcut: Combine both % changes using successive % formula first, then apply directly: x + y - xy/100 style (for mixed profit-loss use appropriate signs).
common_mistakes: Applying second % change on original CP instead of new intermediate price
Sign error when one stage is profit and other is loss

practice_questions.q: A sells to B at 33% profit, B sells to C at 20% loss. C pays ₹532. Find A's CP.

question_types.identification_method: Multiple items bought (often at same/different CP) and sold at different %profit/loss each; overall % asked.
question_types.when_to_use: Calculate Total CP and Total SP separately (sum across all items), then find overall % using totals — never average individual %s directly.
step_by_step: List CP of each article
Apply respective profit/loss% to each CP to get individual SP
Sum all CPs = Total CP; Sum all SPs = Total SP
Overall %=(TotalSP-TotalCP)/TotalCP x100
question_types.shortcut: If CPs are equal for all items, weighted average % works; if CPs differ, MUST use total CP/total SP method.
common_mistakes: Simply averaging the profit/loss percentages when CPs are different
Forgetting an item sold 'at cost price' (0% change) in the total

practice_questions.q: 3 articles bought at ₹3000 each, sold at 10% profit,5% profit,15% loss. Find overall %.
130 books at ₹200 each; half sold at ₹300, 1/5 at ₹350, rest at CP. Find profit%.
Article1 SP=6500 at 4% profit; Article2 CP=3750 sold at 4% loss. Find overall%.

question_types.identification_method: Question says 'gained 1/5 of selling price' or 'profit equal to numerical value of CP' type wording — profit expressed unusually.
question_types.when_to_use: Convert SP-based profit into CP-based ratio: SP=CP+Profit, so if Profit=fraction of SP, derive CP:SP:Profit ratio directly.
step_by_step: Represent SP=Profit+CP as ratio parts (e.g. SP=5 parts, Profit=1 part, CP=4 parts if profit=1/5 of SP)
Profit% on CP = Profit part/CP part x 100
For 'numerical value' type questions, use option-substitution (plug given options into equation)
question_types.shortcut: SP=5,Profit=1,CP=4 pattern for 'profit=1/5 of SP' => profit%=1/4x100=25% directly.
common_mistakes: Directly taking the SP fraction as profit% on CP (wrong base)
Not trying option elimination for 'numerical value equals %' type odd questions

practice_questions.q: Sold scooter for ₹1980, gaining 1/5 of SP. Find profit%.
Sold earphone for ₹2000, profit%=numerical value of CP. Find CP.

question_types.identification_method: Two articles sold at SAME SP, one at x% profit, other at x% loss (same %).
question_types.when_to_use: Direct formula: overall LOSS% = x²/100 — always a loss regardless of x, never profit.
step_by_step: Confirm SP is equal for both articles and %profit=%loss=x (same value)
Apply formula overall loss%=x²/100
If %s are different (not same x), instead find CP of each via SP/(100±x)x100 and compute total CP vs total SP=2xSP
question_types.shortcut: x²/100 formula only works when profit% = loss% (same x); otherwise use full CP:SP calculation.
common_mistakes: Applying x²/100 shortcut even when the two percentages are different values
Assuming overall result could be profit — it is ALWAYS a loss when %s are equal

practice_questions.q: Two articles sold at ₹3000 each, no overall profit/loss. One sold at 25% profit. Find loss% on second.
practice_questions.solution: CP:SP for profit=4:5; for loss found via total=6:5 => loss=1/6x100=16.67%

question_types.identification_method: Mentions shopkeeper using weight less/more than standard (e.g. '950g for 1kg') while claiming cost price or a stated profit%.
question_types.when_to_use: Convert faulty weight into CP:SP ratio directly: CP(true qty):SP(claimed qty).
step_by_step: Identify true weight given (what shopkeeper actually gives) vs standard/claimed weight (what customer pays for)
Form ratio CP:SP = (weight given):(weight paid for) inverted appropriately — CP is based on cost of ACTUAL weight, SP on price of CLAIMED weight
If additional stated profit% exists (selling above cost too), multiply this ratio with the stated profit ratio
Compute profit% from combined ratio
question_types.shortcut: Cheating only while selling using w grams for 1000g claimed: profit%=[(1000-w)/w]x100. If cheating both buying(w1) and selling(w2): combine ratio (1000/w1)x(w2/1000).
common_mistakes: Reversing which quantity is CP and which is SP
Forgetting to combine BOTH buying and selling cheat ratios when both occur
Not incorporating additional explicit profit% stated alongside weight fraud

practice_questions.q: Shopkeeper uses 950g for 1kg claiming cost price. Find gain%.
Cheats 25% while buying, 25% while selling. Find overall profit%.
practice_questions.solution: Buy ratio4:5, Sell ratio3:4; combined CP:SP=3:5=>profit=2/3x100=66.67%(per given example uses different setup: 3:5 =>2/3x100)
practice_questions.answer: See worked method (~66.67%, verify per exact wording)

practice_questions.q: Dealer uses 925g for 1kg, claims cost price. Find gain% (2 decimals).
Shopkeeper has fault of 100g in 3kg weight, sells 3kg potatoes for ₹46, CP=₹12/kg. Find profit.
practice_questions.solution: TotalCP for 2.9kg=34.8; SP=46; but per method: profit=₹11.2

practice_questions.q: Tailor cheats 5cm long while buying, 5cm short while selling (normal=100cm). Find gain%.

question_types.identification_method: Some % of goods gets spoiled/rotten/thrown away before selling; overall profit% given or asked, or selling rate above CP asked.
question_types.when_to_use: Work with total CP for FULL quantity, but SP only from the GOOD (unspoiled) portion sold.
step_by_step: Total CP = full quantity x cost rate
Good quantity = Total quantity x (100-spoilage%)
SP formed only from good quantity at selling rate
Find overall profit% using TotalCP vs TotalSP, or reverse-solve required selling rate/markup
question_types.shortcut: CP:SP ratio shortcut: if spoilage=s%, good%=100-s; required markup% on good units = [Overall desired SP ratio - good% ratio]/good% ratio.
common_mistakes: Using total quantity instead of good quantity when calculating SP
Forgetting spoiled goods still counted in Total CP (money already spent on them)

practice_questions.q: 480 bananas bought for ₹1200, some rotten & thrown away, rest sold at ₹3.5 each, profit=₹396. Find % rotten.
15% items go bad, overall profit=19%. Find markup% on good items sold.

question_types.identification_method: Two different quantities/rates of same commodity mixed together, question asks selling rate for target overall profit%.
question_types.when_to_use: Find weighted average CP of mixture first using (q1r1+q2r2)/(q1+q2), then apply target profit% on this average.
step_by_step: Calculate total cost of both quantities separately
Average CP per unit = Total cost / Total quantity
Apply target profit% formula: Selling rate = Average CP x (100+profit%)/100
question_types.shortcut: Skip finding per-unit average if only overall profit amount needed — directly use TotalCPx(100+p%)/100=TotalSP.
common_mistakes: Averaging the two RATES directly (simple average) instead of weighted average by quantity
Forgetting to add both quantities in denominator

practice_questions.q: 50kg sugar at ₹40/kg mixed with 70kg at ₹30/kg. Find selling rate/kg for 20% profit.
Merchant has 1000kg sugar, part sold at 10% profit, rest at 40% profit, overall gain=20%. Find qty sold at 40% profit.

question_types.type_name: Two Items with Equal Selling Price at Different %s (General)
question_types.identification_method: Two watches/items sold at SAME price, but with DIFFERENT profit% and loss%, CP of one to be found.
question_types.when_to_use: Convert both %s to CP:SP ratios, equalize the SP parts (LCM), then find ratio of CPs.
step_by_step: Convert each %profit/loss to CP:SP ratio
Make SP parts equal (LCM) by scaling both ratios
Now CP ratio directly comparable; use given total CP to find individual CP
question_types.shortcut: Always scale ratios to make SP terms equal before comparing CPs — this is the fastest method for 'equal SP' problems.
common_mistakes: Comparing CP ratios without first equalizing SP parts
Arithmetic error while scaling ratios to common SP

practice_questions.q: Two buffaloes bought for ₹11520 total. One sold at 15% loss, other at 19% profit, SPs equal. Find CP of first.

identification_tricks: Only CP, SP, profit%/loss% mentioned directly — Basic type, use CP:SP ratio.
'A sells to B, B sells to C' — Chain selling type, multiply successive fractions.
Multiple different articles with individual %s — Combined type, use Total CP vs Total SP.
'Gained fraction OF SP' or profit% oddly linked to CP's numerical value — SP-based profit type.
Same SP, same %profit=%loss — apply x²/100 loss shortcut directly.
Weight/measurement fraud mentioned (grams, cm, faulty balance) — Dishonest shopkeeper type.
Spoilage/rotten/thrown away goods mentioned — Perishable goods type, separate Total CP from Good-quantity SP.
Two different rate quantities mixed together — Mixture-based type, weighted average CP.
Two items, equal SP, different %s, CP asked — equalize SP parts method.
solving_approach: Step 1: Identify what is given — CP, SP, MP, profit/loss%, or a ratio/fraction relation.
Step 2: Convert every %profit/loss into a CP:SP ratio immediately (100:100+x or 100:100-x).
Step 3: For multi-stage or multi-item problems, decide whether to use chain multiplication (sequential) or Total CP/Total SP (parallel/combined).
Step 4: For faulty weight or measurement problems, always express as a ratio of (actual quantity):(claimed/paid quantity).
Step 5: Solve using ratio cross-multiplication rather than absolute decimal values wherever possible.
Step 6: Verify sign (profit vs loss) makes logical sense before finalizing answer.
shortcuts: Profit%/Loss% is ALWAYS on CP unless the question explicitly says 'on SP'.
Equal SP + equal %profit/loss(x) on two items = guaranteed overall LOSS of x²/100% — never profit.
Faulty weight while selling only (1000g claimed, w grams given): profit%=(1000-w)/w x100.
For combined transactions, ALWAYS sum Total CP and Total SP separately — never average % values when CPs differ.
'Gained 1/n of SP' pattern: SP=n parts, Profit=1 part, CP=(n-1) parts; profit%=1/(n-1)x100.
common_mistakes: Calculating profit/loss % on SP by default instead of CP.
Averaging profit/loss percentages directly when CPs of items are different.
Reversing CP and SP in faulty weight ratio setup.
Applying x²/100 loss formula when the two percentages are NOT equal.
Forgetting spoiled/rotten quantity still contributes to Total CP even though it generates zero SP.
Using simple average instead of weighted average for mixture CP.
pyq_patterns: Dishonest shopkeeper (faulty weight) questions are asked almost every SSC CGL shift — both single and double cheating (buy+sell) variants.
Combined multi-article profit/loss with different CPs is a recurring Tier-1 favorite testing Total CP/SP method.
'Equal SP, equal %profit=%loss=>loss x²/100' is a guaranteed trap question appearing frequently.
Chain selling (A-B-C) questions combined with successive % changes are common in Tier-2.
Mixture-based profit questions often overlap with 'Mixture and Alligation' chapter — expect cross-topic questions.
quick_revision: Combined transactions: use Total CP vs Total SP, never average %
exam_tips: Always set CP=100 as a base for ratio problems — makes mental math dramatically faster.
For faulty weight questions, draw the CP:SP ratio explicitly on paper — don't rely on mental tracking.
Watch for 'on selling price' wording — it changes the entire base of calculation.
Practice x²/100 shortcut recognition — this exact trap appears almost every exam.
For chain-selling problems, always multiply fractions in the SAME order as the transaction sequence.

one_page_formula_sheet.sp_based_profit: profit=1/n of SP => profit%(on CP)=1/(n-1) x100
